author: @charlestaylor3195
text: I swear this is true, I had a dream, and then I  did the search. My dream went like this: A couple of guys in a helicopter type of fuselage started pedaling, and when they did this big thing on top started spinning. The thing that started spinning wasn't blades, it looked like a big  T shape rotating and on the arms of the T were rotating cylinders, but the cylinders weren't flat and smooth they were several long blades sort of joined together on one edge horizontally. So they pedaled at a constant rate that was so slow it created no lift, then they flicked a switch and the cylinders started rotating at a high variable speeds, but the rotating T remained at the same speed. I could hear the rpms of the cylinders increase and decrease as they maneuvered. Maybe I'm missing some physics principals, then again dreams have ways of defying physics. I believe the magnus effect. I've seen where they can adjust the pitch of the blades in the rotating cylinders to manipulate flight, but I've never seen the cylinders rotated horizontally. Just an idea I though I'd shoot your way, you are definitely more able to experiment with something like that than I am. This is a totally  different take on the effect you are doing in this video.

author: @dnomyarnostaw
text:
This is a really late contribution,  but the Japanese did NOT invent the Flettner Rotor Windmill.
In fact, Anton Flettner himself designed one.
And Popular Mechanics magazine in the 1950s showed a 30 ft working example. 
And this version is derivative of about 6 other experiments I have seen .
The true innovation of Japanese version of the Flettner Rotor windmill  for hurricanes had spiral flutes running up the cylinder.

author: @CG-cw4lk
text: Delving into the Magnus effect and Flettner rotors again has brought to mind a really BIG idea for electricity generation by the rotor. It was a 20mw plant that was to be built at Burlington New Jersey USA in 1933. The Madras rotor, as it was called, was to run on a circular railway under its own force effect, having its wheels to be geared up to a generator. But even though a rotor was built and tested, it all came to nothing. I can only find one reference to the Madras rotor idea on the web, there used to be more.

author: @CG-cw4lk
text:
A one megawatt horizontal wind turbine was built in Poland, the Acowind A63 around the turn of the century, there are photos on the net. It didn't live up to expectations, what happen to I don't know, but I kept an eye on information it about while it was working. Magnus effect wind turbines, whether hwt it vwt, are better used as pumps, so, if I remember correctly, the A63 used an oil transmission to hydraulically pump round the generator, probably another idea on paper that didn't work in practice - the Enfield Cables Andreus wind turbine is another example of not living up to engineers paperwork hopes.
What is more to the point is the fact that many years before the A63, the man most closely associated with the application of the Magnus effect, Atom Flettner, built a similar size hwt, which must have been the largest wind turbine of the day. I think the lack of knowledge of this is due to the fact that there are no photos of this turbine on the web, although there are some line drawings. I have seen a photo of this turbine because I have read The English translation of Flettener's biography, The Road To The Rotor, published in 1926.The turbine was totally unfit for purpose, the four rotors,NSEW, we're in front of a control cabin with a gantry between. It was more like a building than like the industrial wind turbines of today, it could never be high enough to catch the wind it needed.
But Flettner never really intended the turbine's main blades to be connected to the generator, he intended to put a smaller turbine on the end of each rotor blade and use the higher tip speed for electricity generation. There's a drawing of this type of turbine in the biography.
I hope some publisher one day will publish this biography, it's out of copyright, so it shouldn't cost too much. Or if perhaps somebody has an old cop they could scan for us, he was an enterprising inventor, it's a good read.

author: @jackieadams8153
text: This does not demonstrate the Magnus effect. To demonstrate it properly you would need to power rotate each cylinder. The direction of rotation would need to change depending on which half of the rotation it was on so the force created is in the direction of the rotating plate. Also it would work better if you had rough cylinders instead of smooth ones.

author: @justinw1765
text:
The funny thing is, if you look at a broad sweep of history, all the major paradigm shifting and society affecting inventors, scientists, etc, all had very conventional, closed minded, majority/consensus thinkers tell them that their new and/or unique invention or theory was completely wrong, would never work, etc.  

    There are so many examples to pull from, from the Wright brothers, to Tesla, to Einstein, then to the original Quantum physics researchers, etc, etc.  

  There is a clear pattern of more fixed, closed minded, fear based, lacking in imagination types, telling people with broader, more insightful, more imaginative, more intuitive people that they are stupid, these things can't be done, etc.  

     It seems kind of obvious then, that keeping one's mind at least slightly open, is probably actually a practical/pragmatic approach to engage in.

author: @wilfrieddebruyne9212
text: Hi Robert, I am very open minded bud..... this is not a Magnus effect generator drive. The Magnus effect requires the cylinder to spin. Your rotor spins because the blower is positioned offset. Now it is rotating counterclockwise if you install the blower offset on the other side it will turn clockwise. You know that. I am looking forward to see version 2.0

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: Spot on, this really is misinformation, no magnus effect, and its not a wind turbine. I too look forward to version 2, with powered cylinders so we can see the real magnus effect. It would need a controller and a battery so it can start up, and the 3 motors will impact heavily on any generated power, a turbine of that size is unlikely to generate more power than it uses to spin the rotors.

replies.author: @EnvenomatedNickels
replies.text: I've had a similar idea for a while.  Additionally you could have a bicycle powered alternator hooked up to a generator.  Have it all on a platform like a gazebo where you go out, exercise on the generator bike while shaded by solar panels and wind blows through to cool you off and also power the turbines mounted in the supporting pillars.   The issues I had conceptually were 1) weatherproofing and 2) battery charging circuit design.  It would have to be weatherproofed since it would be an outdoor structure year round.  The circuitry part I imagine I would need one or several charge controllers  and/or multiple batteries to manage the different input voltages but perhaps this could be managed with just diode rectifiers.  Cheers!

author: @goldmagnet9013
text: Rob (or anyone) can you explain how the magnus effect applies in your design ? The design you are replication has electric motors in the cylinders so they spin and can take advantage of the magnus effect. In your design there are no motors, the cylinders are not spinning, so no magnus effect ? I assume yours spins because the fan is not placed centrally. I do not belive you have replicated the design as you said at the start, nor do I believe there is any magnus effect taking place as you are not spinning the cylinders at all. I worry you may take this video down and just say there were negative comments, but please educate me, how is there any magnus effect ? Why does the Japanese design use complex and expensive electric motors to spin the cylinders when yours does it without ? The cylinders have to be spun up.... they dont spin as a result of airflow past them.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text:
Here is the quote from the designers website: "In the vertical-axis Magnus wind turbine, the cylindrical blades are driven by an electric motor, which causes self-consumption of electric power. The net power generation amount is the amount of power generated minus the power consumption of the motor, so the power generation efficiency is inferior to existing wind power generators. On the other hand, the Magnus wind turbine can start power generation at a wind speed of 4 m/sec and continue power generation up to a maximum wind speed of 40 m/sec. Compared to the existing wind power generation that can only generate wind speeds of up to 25 m/sec, the wind speed range in which power can be generated is greatly expanded. As a result, the operating time will be longer and the capacity utilization of the generator will be improved, so the greatest feature is that the total power generation amount exceeds the power generation efficiency that is inferior to the existing wind turbine.
Currently, we are acquiring data on power generation efficiency and facility utilization rate in a verification test in Ishigaki City, Okinawa Prefecture, which started in August 2018."

replies.author: @petersharp6833
replies.text: @goldmagnet9013 Your are correct. This is not a Magnus rotor -- for the reasons you stated. I've done a lot of experimenting with the Magnus effect and also with various types of VAWT, and I've read lots of research papers on the subject.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Years ago I seen a Magnus effect horizontal axis wind turbine.  Seeing a vertical axis wind turbine should have been obvious, and it is kind of neat.  

Although, the magnus effect is supose to be arodynamic lift on spinning objects (cylinder sphere, ect) in direction tangently to the wind.  Meaning the cylinders should already be spinning.

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text:
That was very pleasing to see.  Some of the best inventions have come from things other people have rubbished in the past.
Always keep an open mind, if someone can't get it to work, it does not mean that someone else won't and sometimes it an imperfection of someone else trying that get it to go.  So before anyone rubbishes anything, go try it for yourself, you never know, you might be the one that make it work better than anyone else ever has.  I wish I have the resources and time to play with half the things I see here and elsewhere.

I feel a little confused, how is this the Magnus effect? Aren't you just pointing the blower at one side of it?
I can't see how this would work (as it is) in normal wind. Wouldn't the cylinders need to be rapidly rotating to cause a Magnus effect?
Perhaps I'm misunderstanding something!?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: have a read the Japanese research mate

replies.author: @GofuKyersen
replies.text: Robert said the blower was aimed dead centre in the video mate.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: I think Rob messed up here. The original Japanese design has electric motors to spin up the cylinders, I think Rob missed this point. His design does not replicate the original, does not take advantage of the Magnus effect (as you say), and I agree the fan is off centre, its closer to him in the demo than the centre of the "turbine". Its make believe.

replies.author: @littleworkshopofhorrors2395
replies.text: I just watched a vid of the hopefully commercial product from Japan and sure enough they state the cylinders are powered to create the effect.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: @littleworkshopofhorrors2395 Puts Robs testing and knowledge under the spotlight somewhat, there are people commenting on here that they are going to build his "awesome" machine ! Its wrong to encourage people to build this design.

author: @eleventeenmachine5991
text: The thing about vertical axis turbines that I see as problematic is that they are easily clogged up with debris in high winds. So in a rooftop setup that's lower than the tallest tree around it's liable to catch a branch. Once that happens your balance is toast. You're drag is no longer equal. And now it's not spinning so the deflection of debris and or the centrifugal self cleaning is no more. Next thing you know, you have a mess on the roof that looks like Swamp Thing.

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It would be so awesome to see you replicating some of Alvin M Marks legendary electrostatic generators. He was a most brilliant outside the box thinker, innovator & engineer, that unfortunately not many know about in this new era. You could say he was an inventor a bit too far ahead of his time a bit like Nikola Tesla. I very much believe many of his ground breaking inventions could now have a vastly positive impact for low cost virtually solid state sustainable power generation & energy storage. Some of them also have theoretical energy densities and efficiencies far exceeding anything we have today, for solid state electrostatic thermal energy conversion to his highly futuristic 1-15 kwhr/kg Quensor tech, which are well worth looking at to inspire us today :) P

author: @goldmagnet9013
text: I dont know what a hurricane device is,  do hurricane devices only produce power in hurricane class winds ? If so Hurricane devices have to be the worst idea for power generation. Generate 2 or 3 days per year, maybe none, and this generation happens when you need power least (most businesses would close in a Hurricane).. As Rob has said the energy storage is a big issue for renewable s, and and having a system that works a few days per year presents storage issues too (batteries like being charged more often than once a year).   I live in a high wind site and have had turbines for 20 + years, there are already great solutions for high wind sites (antarctic, north sea etc), I just dont get it other than its an interesting effect.
